common_name: Harry Lauder's Walking Stick (Twisted Corkscrew Hazel, Contorted Filbert)

planting.note: Plant grafted nursery trees in early spring or fall; Harry Lauder's Walking Stick is a deciduous ornamental shrub with one of the most dramatic branch structures available in temperate horticulture — the stems twist and contort in spiraling, corkscrew patterns that become the primary ornamental feature during the leafless winter months (November–March in zone 9), when the gnarled, spiral branch architecture is fully visible and at its most striking. The contorted growth form originated as a chance mutation in a European hazel hedge in England and was named for Sir Harry Lauder, a Scottish entertainer known for carrying a contorted walking stick cane. In zone 9, Harry Lauder's Walking Stick thrives as a permanent landscape shrub, leafing out in spring with typical hazel foliage and producing ornamental yellow catkins in late winter (January–February) before the leaves emerge — the catkins are particularly charming hanging from the contorted bare branches. The plant is slow-growing and typically reaches 8–10 feet over 10–15 years.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil with morning sun to light shade. Prune out any straight-growing rootstock shoots that emerge below the graft. Harry Lauder's Walking Stick (Corylus avellana 'Contorta') is one of the most prized ornamental shrubs in temperate horticulture, valued primarily for the extraordinary architectural quality of its winter branch structure. The plant is propagated by grafting the contorted cultivar onto straight European hazel (Corylus avellana) rootstock, and all woody growth above the graft union will be contorted; any growth from below the graft union will be normal straight hazel and must be removed as it appears. The contorted growth habit affects every branch and twig, producing a dense winter silhouette of spiraling, twisting branches that catches and holds frost, snow, and ice dramatically and creates complex shadow patterns on winter walls and garden surfaces. When bare of leaves, a mature Harry Lauder's Walking Stick is one of the most winter-interesting plants in temperate gardening — its corkscrew branches have inspired garden designers for generations as structural focal points in winter gardens. In spring, the contorted branches are draped with drooping yellow catkins (male flowers) before the large, slightly crinkled, typical hazel leaves emerge, adding a brief period of early-spring charm before the plant becomes a handsome but unremarkable leafy shrub for the rest of the growing season. The plant produces small nuts in fall, but nut production is typically sparse compared to standard hazel varieties.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Corylus avellana 'Contorta' cultivation notes
Michael A. Dirr — Manual of Woody Landscape Plants (ornamental shrub documentation)
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Harry Lauder's Walking Stick species profile

common_name: Kleim's Hardy Gardenia (Compact Dwarf Cold-Hardy Gardenia for Zone 6-9 Landscapes)

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring or early fall; Kleim's Hardy Gardenia is a dwarf compact cultivar of the standard gardenia, reaching only 2–3 feet tall and 3–4 feet wide — far smaller than the 4–6 foot standard gardenias commonly grown in zone 9 landscapes. The compact habit makes it practical for foundation plantings, container culture, low hedges, and smaller garden spaces where standard gardenias would overwhelm the setting. The variety produces single (not double) white flowers — smaller than standard gardenia blooms but produced prolifically and with the same intensely sweet, classic gardenia fragrance that makes these plants irreplaceable in Southern gardens. In zone 9, Kleim's Hardy is evergreen and fully cold-hardy, tolerating winter temperatures well below those that damage standard gardenias; its hardiness extends to zone 6b–7, making it one of the cold-hardiest gardenias available. Plant in partial shade for best performance in zone 9 — full afternoon sun in summer can cause leaf yellowing (iron chlorosis) and flower bleaching.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil with morning sun or light shade. Avoid alkaline soil and overhead irrigation. Kleim's Hardy Gardenia is one of the most recommended landscape gardenias for the Gulf South, combining the irreplaceable fragrance and white-flower beauty of the classic gardenia with superior cold hardiness, compact growth, and adaptability to typical Gulf Coast landscape conditions. Standard gardenias (Gardenia jasminoides selections like 'August Beauty' and 'Mystery') are among the most beloved and frequently requested landscape shrubs in zone 9, valued for their intensely fragrant white flowers that perfume entire gardens and homes in late spring. However, standard gardenias reach 4–6 feet in height and width, require specific acidic soil conditions, and can be damaged or killed by unusually cold winters in the upper zones of their range. Kleim's Hardy addresses several of these limitations: its compact 2–3 foot stature fits gardens and foundation plantings where standard sizes would be inappropriate; its hardiness to zone 6b–7 provides additional cold protection that allows it to be grown successfully further north and to survive zone 9 cold snaps that occasionally damage standard varieties; and its prolific single-flower production maintains the fragrance that makes gardenias essential in Southern gardens. The single flower form of Kleim's Hardy is considered by some gardeners more elegant than the full double flowers of standard varieties — the simpler, five-petaled white flower has a purity of form that the multi-petaled doubles lack. Gardenias require strongly acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.0) and are heavy iron users; supplemental acidic fertilizer and occasional chelated iron application prevents the characteristic yellowing between leaf veins (iron chlorosis) that affects gardenias in near-neutral or alkaline soils.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Gardenia Cultivar Recommendations for Gulf Coast Landscapes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Gardenia jasminoides in Florida Landscapes
Michael A. Dirr — Dirr's Hardy Trees and Shrubs (Gardenia jasminoides 'Kleim's Hardy' documentation)

common_name: Sugar Dumpling Squash (Small Sweet Winter Squash with Cream-and-Green Stripes)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; Sugar Dumpling is a miniature winter squash producing 1–2 pound individual fruits about 4 inches in diameter — the personal serving size of the squash is one of its primary practical advantages, as each fruit is exactly right for halving and stuffing or baking as a single portion without the waste of cutting a large squash. The striped cream-and-green skin pattern closely resembles the Delicata/Sweet Dumpling type but Sugar Dumpling produces distinctly smaller, rounder, slightly more compact fruits. In zone 9, plant March–May for summer production and again in late July–August for fall harvest. The flesh is deep orange-yellow, very sweet (high in natural sugars), and dry-textured with excellent baking quality. Sugar Dumpling's compact vining habit (4–5 foot vines) is more manageable than large winter squash varieties.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 36–48 inches. Harvest when skin shows orange or cream color without green and stem begins to dry. Sugar Dumpling Squash is a miniature winter squash variety in the Cucurbita pepo species that combines the sweet, dry, chestnut-flavored flesh of the best small winter squash types with an individual serving size (1–2 lbs, 4 inches diameter) that makes it supremely practical for home cooking. Unlike large winter squash varieties that require days of eating to finish once cut, Sugar Dumpling's small fruits provide a single meal's worth of squash for 2–4 people when halved and baked — eliminating the challenge of storing cut large squash and the quality loss that comes with refrigerating cut raw winter squash. The skin is a distinctive cream-white with thin green stripes that transition as the squash matures, eventually showing more orange-cream at full maturity (a ripeness indicator). The flesh is deep orange-yellow, very sweet, with the dense, dry texture that characterizes the best winter squash for baking and stuffing rather than the watery texture of summer squash types. Sugar Dumpling's vines are shorter (4–5 feet) than large pumpkin or Hubbard varieties, making the variety more practical for smaller garden spaces. For zone 9 fall production, plants started in late July can produce ripe squash by October–November, well-timed for the fall harvest and holiday table.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Sugar Dumpling mini winter squash variety notes
Burpee Seeds — Sugar Dumpling compact winter squash growing guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Winter Squash Variety Selection for Gulf South Production

common_name: Dark Star Zucchini (Near-Black Dark-Striped Hybrid Zucchini with Superior Heat Tolerance)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Dark Star is a hybrid zucchini notable for its extremely dark green to near-black fruit color, vigorous plant habit, and better-than-average heat tolerance in warm climates. In zone 9, plant March–May for spring production and again in August–September for fall production. The fruit color — very dark green with fine lighter green speckles or striping — is among the darkest of any common zucchini variety, a contrast to the medium green of standard zucchini. The very dark color is more visually striking at farmers markets and in salads. Dark Star was recognized as an AAS winner for its outstanding performance in trials across diverse growing conditions, including warm-summer locations where many zucchini varieties decline earlier in the season. Harvest at 6–8 inches for best flavor; the skin remains tender and the flesh dense at this size.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 24–36 inches. Harvest at 6–8 inches when skin is uniformly very dark green. Dark Star Zucchini is an F1 hybrid summer squash variety developed for its dramatically dark fruit color, vigor, and heat performance in warm-summer conditions — qualities that make it particularly appropriate for Gulf Coast zone 9 gardens where summer zucchini production is challenging due to the combination of heat, powdery mildew pressure, and vine borer activity. Standard zucchini typically produces well in spring (March–May in zone 9) and again in fall (August–October) but struggles during the July–August peak summer period. Dark Star's improved heat tolerance extends the productive period somewhat beyond what standard green zucchini varieties manage, though no summer squash is fully tolerant of zone 9 peak summer conditions. The fruit's near-black to very dark green color (produced by high concentrations of chlorophyll and other pigments in the skin) is the variety's most immediately distinctive characteristic — a mature Dark Star fruit at 6–8 inches harvest size is substantially darker than a standard green zucchini, with a glossy, nearly black-green skin that stands out dramatically when displayed with lighter-colored vegetables. The flesh is standard zucchini quality — white, mild, with good flavor at proper harvest size — with no meaningful flavor difference from standard green varieties. The AAS recognition for Dark Star reflected consistent performance advantages in vine health, production duration, and uniformity across trial locations including warmer growing regions.
sources: All-America Selections — Dark Star zucchini AAS recognition and performance documentation
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Dark Star hybrid zucchini variety notes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Summer Squash Variety Recommendations for Gulf South

common_name: Brown Select Satsuma (Premier Gulf Coast Satsuma Mandarin, Louisiana Commercial Standard)

planting.note: Plant grafted nursery trees in spring after frost risk passes; Brown Select Satsuma is one of the most important and widely grown satsuma mandarin selections for the Gulf Coast region, particularly in Louisiana, Mississippi, and southern Alabama where it was selected and evaluated for superior performance under Gulf Coast conditions. It ripens earlier than Owari (the other leading Gulf Coast satsuma) and produces exceptionally sweet, rich juice with the characteristic easy-peel, seedless Satsuma profile. The trees are moderately cold-hardy (tolerating temperatures to around 28°F briefly without major damage) and perform best in zone 8b–9b. Brown Select is named for L.H. Brown, the Louisiana citrus enthusiast who selected it. In zone 9, the fruit typically ripens October–November, making it one of the earliest fresh-tree-ripened citrus available in the season. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant grafted trees in well-drained, slightly acidic soil with full sun. Protect from frost below 28°F by covering young trees. Harvest October–November when fully orange. Brown Select Satsuma is a mandarin orange selection developed and popularized in Louisiana, where Satsuma mandarins have been grown commercially and in home gardens along the Gulf Coast since Japanese immigrants introduced the variety to Louisiana in the late 19th century. Satsuma mandarins (Citrus reticulata, specifically the 'Unshiu' or 'Satsuma' group) became an integral part of Gulf Coast culture — Louisiana's St. Bernard Parish and adjacent areas were once a significant commercial Satsuma production center, and Satsuma season (October–December) remains a beloved Gulf South annual event with home trees producing for family and community distribution. The name Satsuma comes from the Satsuma Province of Japan, where the variety was cultivated for centuries before reaching the United States. Brown Select's primary distinction from the more widely distributed Owari Satsuma is earlier ripening (Brown Select ripens 2–3 weeks earlier, in October rather than November) and what many Gulf Coast growers consider superior juice quality — slightly richer, sweeter, and with more complex flavor than Owari in typical Gulf Coast growing conditions. The tree is vigorous and productive under good culture, reliably producing heavy crops of 2-inch flattened-round fruits with loose, easy-peel skin, seedless flesh (typically zero to few seeds per fruit), and intensely sweet, juicy mandarin character. For zone 9 gardeners seeking a productive citrus tree, Brown Select Satsuma is often the first recommendation — earlier than most other mandarins, reliably productive, cold-tolerant enough for zone 9, and producing fruit of exceptional quality.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Satsuma Mandarin Variety Selection for Louisiana
Southern Living Garden — Gulf Coast Satsuma citrus culture and Brown Select variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Satsuma Mandarin Culture for Gulf Coast Home Gardens
